# Questions in Livestream 240

Live Stream #240: The Last of Winter's Tea

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=346" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:46</a> | What is Boxing Day? | He jokes that it is a retail holiday and notes it is associated with tipping doormen in some places. | He calls Boxing Day a fake retail holiday used by Canadian retailers, then adds that in some places it is also associated with tipping doormen. He places its origin in the 19th century and treats it mostly as seasonal channel chatter before moving on. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:43</a> | Will that coffee maker work on electric stoves? | Yes on a standard electric heating element, though he is unsure about induction stoves. | He says the stovetop coffee maker will work on his own electric stove because it has a conventional heating element. He does not know whether it will work on an induction stove, so his answer is limited to ordinary electric burners. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=1156" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:16</a> | Do Sudan hieroglyphs exist? | Yes. He says they copy Egyptian hieroglyphs, but the underlying language is still not understood well enough to translate confidently. | He says Sudanese or Nubian hieroglyphs do exist, especially in the region from the second to fourth cataracts. They look like Egyptian hieroglyphs because the script was copied from Egypt, but he compares the translation problem to Egyptology before Champollion because the underlying language is still uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=1253" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:53</a> | Do you have an Amazon wish list for people who want to send gifts? | Not yet, but they say one may be put together and that Amazon.ca works best for sending things to them in Canada. | He says they do not yet have a wish list because it is difficult to assemble, but Mrs. Producer says she will put one together. They ask people to use Amazon.ca for shipping to Canada because it avoids border, tariff, and delivery complications. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=1310" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:50</a> | So I cannot order you a whole roll of cheese? | He says people can send things like cheese if they order through Amazon.ca. | He says cheese is fine if ordered through Amazon.ca, joking that they have storage for it and would also appreciate cheese and sausage because of their ketogenic diet. The key point is that Canadian Amazon is the practical route for sending food gifts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=1537" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:37</a> | Do you live in Canada or South America? | He answers that he lives in Canada and compares current Canadian conditions unfavorably to South America. | He says he lives in Canada, then complains that Canada is becoming more like South America in political and speech conditions. He connects that comment to government pressure, tariffs, and what he sees as attempts to strip away religious liberties. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=1628" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:08</a> | Is Canada really bad for Christians? | He says it is getting that way and points to declining freedoms and government pressure. | He says Canada is getting bad for Christians, citing lack of response to violence against churches and broader loss of freedom. He points to Canada's drop in a human freedom index as evidence that government control is increasing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=1702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:22</a> | If spirits and the divine council are called sons of God in Job and Psalm 82, why does Hebrews 1:5 seem to deny it? | He says Hebrews is using son in a different sense, as an inheritor, rather than denying the other passages. | He says Hebrews 1:5 is not denying Job or Psalm 82 but using "son" with a different nuance. In Hebrews, sonship refers to declaration, inheritance, and designated heirship, whereas Job and Psalm 82 use the phrase more generally for created heavenly beings or, in Psalm 82, possibly also human judges. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=1876" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:16</a> | Is the honorable-mentioned best villain character the one based on the scumbag youth pastor? | No. He says that figure belongs more to the second book, while the first-book villain is the bullying mission commander. | He says the character based on the youth pastor appears more in the second book. The honorable-mention villain being discussed belongs to the first book and is the incompetent mission commander, an ex-football-captain type who bullies his way through command. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=2209" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:49</a> | The Amarna period has many administrative documents. Why did we not find papyrus documents from Amarna? | He says Amarna was seasonally too wet and low-lying, so papyrus rotted away, while the surviving Amarna archive is mainly foreign diplomatic cuneiform tablets. | He corrects the premise, saying Amarna does not preserve many administrative documents; what survives is mostly diplomatic correspondence in Akkadian cuneiform, with a few other languages. Papyrus is rare because Amarna sat low enough for seasonal flooding and moisture to destroy Egyptian papyri, unlike very dry preservation contexts such as tombs or Deir el-Medina. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=2481" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:21</a> | Do you remember the thing about the 30 pieces of silver? | He links it to Exodus 21:32, notes the payment was in shekels rather than denarii, and says it carried symbolic weight as the price tied to a slain servant. | He connects the figure to Exodus 21:32, where 30 pieces of silver is the blood-money payment for a slave gored by an ox. He stresses that Judas was paid in shekels, not denarii, making it a sizable sum, while also seeing symbolic force because Christ is the suffering servant and the payment resembles the legal price for a slain servant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=2741" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:41</a> | Given what you said about David Wood's paper at ETS, is there anyone more reliable you would recommend on Islam? | He says Islam is not his field, though he cautiously points to Imad Eddin Ibrahim as a serious scholar. | He repeatedly says he is not an expert on Islam and cannot give strong guidance. The only name he cautiously suggests is Imad Eddin Ibrahim, whom he describes as a bona fide scholar on Islam and the person who critiqued Wood, but he frames even that as outside his own specialization. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=2955" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:15</a> | I ran into an argument that having communion every week is wrong because it would become cultish. Thoughts? | He rejects that argument, saying regular communion is not demonic or empty by definition and that ritual itself is not inherently bad. | He rejects the objection as badly formed, especially if "cultish" is meant to imply demonic. He argues that ritual is not inherently evil, that Christianity has prescribed rituals, and that doing the Lord's Supper weekly or even more often does not make it demonic or empty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=3455" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:35</a> | How do you explain to a fundamentalist that honoring your parents means not abandoning them in old age? | He says the command is aimed mainly at adult children caring for vulnerable parents, not at little children obeying household rules. | He says the commandment to honor father and mother is directed primarily to adults, especially adult sons in the original social setting. Its point is that parents cared for children when they were vulnerable, so adult children must not leave aging parents destitute or uncared for. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=3603" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:03</a> | What if your parents did not take care of you? | He says you honor them appropriately, with boundaries when needed, rather than becoming a doormat for abusive parents. | He says neglected or abusive parents should still be honored in the way God would want, but that does not require becoming a doormat. Boundaries, refusing manipulative demands, and declining harmful family situations can be part of honoring them appropriately. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=3659" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:59</a> | Are we permitted to send elderly parents to nursing homes? | Yes, if they cannot safely care for themselves, but children still bear responsibility to choose and monitor a decent home. | He says nursing homes can be appropriate when parents cannot care for themselves or when conditions such as dementia exceed what adult children can handle. However, the child remains responsible to choose a humane, competent home, visit, ask questions, and avoid simply abandoning relatives to poor care. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=3800" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:20</a> | What about a nursing home with a funeral home attached? | He would avoid that unless it is really hospice-style care, and even then he advises caution. | He says he would probably avoid a nursing home attached to a funeral home unless it is actually a hospice arrangement for end-of-life palliative care. Even then, he says such a setup calls for careful due diligence because ordinary nursing homes and hospices serve different purposes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=4047" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:27</a> | Explain the Christian origin of tarot cards. | He says early tarot functioned as a visual teaching tool used by monks to present the gospel before it was later co-opted for divination. | He says early tarot decks were used by monks as a visual teaching aid for illiterate audiences, with ordered images explaining creation, fall, crucifixion, and consummation. In his account, the cards were later co-opted for divination, but older decks still preserve traces of Christian symbolism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=4380" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:00</a> | Did you all write today? Progress is progress. | Yes. He says he wrote about 500 words that day and about 4,000 words over the week. | He says he did write that day, about 500 words, and that the week totaled roughly 4,000 words. The answer treats even modest daily progress as meaningful movement on the writing project. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=4429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:49</a> | Why does the Western world seem less receptive to the spiritual world? Has modern science and secularism dulled our awareness of the divine? | He blames modernity, naturalism, and the Enlightenment's mechanical worldview for training Westerners to read reality in less spiritual terms. | He traces Western spiritual dullness to naturalism, naive scientific realism, and the mechanical worldview associated with Enlightenment thought. He says the West learned to view reality as a closed mechanism governed by physical law, leaving God as a distant deistic clockmaker rather than a present divine actor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=4786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:46</a> | Why did some bystanders at Jesus's crucifixion think he was calling for Elijah in Matthew 27:46? | He says some expected Elijah to return with divine aid for the Messiah and misunderstood Jesus's cry through that lens. | He says some bystanders heard Jesus's cry through expectations that Elijah would return to prepare or aid the Messiah. Since they could not yet grasp that Jesus was dying as the sacrificial lamb, they interpreted the cry as a request for divine rescue or military vindication. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=4915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:55</a> | Did they not know their own language? | He says they did, but they were interpreting Jesus's cry through expectations about Elijah, Messiah, and divine aid. | He says the issue was not linguistic ignorance. They knew the language, but interpreted the words through a zealot-shaped expectation that Messiah would call for Elijah, angelic aid, or divine intervention to defeat Rome rather than die for sins. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=5146" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:46</a> | What are some good resources on the environmental state of the Sinai around the time of the Exodus? | He says this work is still emerging and is best found in recent academic and conference papers rather than a settled popular corpus. | He says the work is mostly in academic and conference papers because the field is still forming. He notes that scholars only recently began applying environmental and archaeobotanical questions to the Sinai and nearby regions, challenging older assumptions that the Sinai and Jordan were always desert-like. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=5740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:40</a> | How far of a drive is it to the US border? | He says it is roughly an hour from where he lives, though he rarely makes the trip by car. | He says the drive from where he lives to the US border is roughly an hour, taking him toward Blaine. He rarely does it by car because he does not own a vehicle, car sharing cannot cross the border, and other routes such as the train can be easier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=5886" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:06</a> | Do you get groceries delivered, or is stuff within walking distance? | His usual grocery store is about four blocks away, so he normally walks and only gets heavier loads delivered. | He says his main grocery store is about four blocks away, so he walks there several times a week and carries groceries home in a backpack. Delivery is reserved for bulky or heavy purchases. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=6046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:46</a> | What is Montanism? | He describes it as the claim that new revelations given to Montanus and his prophetesses carried divine authority alongside scripture. | He defines formal Montanism as the movement around Montanus and his two prophetesses, who claimed direct revelations from the Holy Spirit with divine authority. He also uses "informal Montanism" for any claim that direct personal messages from God carry the same weight as scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=6101" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:41</a> | What does religion mean in James 1:26? Does it have the same connotations as today? | He says the term is still basically religion, but James stresses lived practice rather than mere profession. | He says the Greek term is a general word for religion and has broadly similar connotations to modern usage. James is not saying religion is bad; he is saying faith must be lived out in practice, including bridling the tongue, caring for widows and orphans, and remaining unstained by the world. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=6388" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:28</a> | James 1:19: should we be quick to forgive? | He says believers should be quick to forgive, while also distinguishing forgiveness from merely letting an offense go. | He says James 1:19 does not directly discuss forgiveness, but Christians should infer the need to be quick to forgive because God forgave them. He distinguishes actual forgiveness, which involves repentance and reconciliation, from merely choosing not to carry an offense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=6786" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:06</a> | Will the offended party be emotionally recovered once in heaven? | Yes. He says Christ will heal those hurts, even if the pain can linger in this life. | He says heaven includes the healing of every hurt, citing the tree of life and the healing of the nations. In this life, the offended person may still carry the wound even after forgiving, but Christ ultimately heals those harms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=7125" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:45</a> | Is asking for forgiveness somewhat risky? | Yes. He says the offended person might refuse, but Christians should still be ready to seek and extend forgiveness. | He says asking forgiveness is risky because the offended person can refuse or seek revenge. Even so, Christians should be quick to forgive because the Lord was quick to forgive them, while still distinguishing forgiveness from unhealthy passivity when someone remains unrepentant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=7299" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:39</a> | Was Haman impaled in Esther? | Yes. He says the scaffold in Esther refers to a Persian-style impalement stake. | He says Esther's "scaffold" is better understood as an impalement pole, a Persian execution method also known earlier among Assyrians. In his reading, Haman prepared that death for others and then died by the same instrument himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=7506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:06</a> | How do we know David was in his 50s with Bathsheba? | He says the narrative places David at an age where he was no longer fit for frontline battle and had already been told to stay back. | He says the text describes David as old enough that he became a liability on the battlefield when fighting the sons of the giant. His men had to protect him instead of focusing on the enemy, so he was told to retire from frontline battle, which explains why he was in the palace while others went to war. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=7680" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:00</a> | I have heard sermons say David's place was fighting with his men. Is that not so? | He says no, because David had already become a liability in battle and had been pulled from that role. | He says those sermons miss the narrative context: David was not shirking a proper combat duty because he had already been pulled from battlefield service. The point is not that his place was with the army, but that he was too old or vulnerable for that role. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=7692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:12</a> | How could Caleb and Joshua still fight at older ages if David was too old to fight? | He says the comparison is weak because the conquest involved smaller siege campaigns, while David faced large Philistine field battles. | He says Joshua and Caleb fought in a different military context: conquest warfare against Amorite city-states was often smaller, siege-oriented, and incremental. David's setting involved large Philistine field armies fighting over the Shephelah, where a weakened king could be killed like Saul and become a strategic disaster. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=8030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:50</a> | What is your theory as to why God told the Israelites not to eat pork? | He thinks the ban was tied mainly to agricultural and water-fouling concerns rather than to disease alone. | He argues the original concern was likely agricultural: pig farming fouled canals and waterways, a serious problem in Egypt and in hill-country drainage systems. He treats disease concerns such as trichinosis as secondary or modernly misunderstood, noting that ancient pig husbandry was the bigger practical problem. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=8284" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:04</a> | So how did East Asia deal with it, since they love pork? | He says they tolerated the smell and notes that the trichinosis issue he mentioned was not the central old-world factor. | He says East Asia essentially put up with the stench of pig farming. He also argues that trichinosis was not the old-world reason for avoiding pork, claiming it was mainly a New World problem and that the pork question was more about agriculture and sanitation than parasite fear. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=8439" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:39</a> | What did he think of the Mesopotamia book AJ Higgins mentioned? | He says he had not read it and would not evaluate it on that basis. | He declines to comment because he has not read or investigated the book. He says he only comments on books he has at least looked into or read himself, so he gives this one a pass. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=8520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:00</a> | Any new developments coming from Shiloh? | He says the dig is still active, but he has not seen a full field report and thinks the team may be cautious about publishing some dating claims. | He says ABR is still digging at Shiloh, but he has not seen a proper field report and doubts when it will appear. He thinks the team is cautious because Bronze Age dating claims could be challenged in peer review, though he notes they continue to report architecture and had at least some worthwhile papers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=8677" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:37</a> | Would I have to abandon milk if I went on keto? | Yes. He says regular milk is too high in lactose, though cheese and some substitutes may still work. | He says regular milk is off a ketogenic diet because lactose is sugar. Cream, almond milk, soy milk, or other low-sugar substitutes may work, and cheese is acceptable because it does not carry the same lactose load as milk. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=8723" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:23</a> | How reputable is ABR Associates for Research? | He says they are capable excavators but not trustworthy interpreters of their own data because ideology skews their reporting. | He distinguishes ABR's field skill from its interpretation. He says they are organized and capable excavators, but their commitment to an early Exodus date leads them to distort or redate evidence, citing Jericho and the Mount Ebal curse amulet as examples of why archaeologists are cautious about them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=9043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:30:43</a> | What do you put in your coffee? | Nothing. He says he drinks it black. | He answers simply that he puts nothing in his coffee and drinks it black. He then jokes that the caffeine must flow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=9084" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:24</a> | Do you fear each other dying, how do you deal with it, and what happens to married couples in the hereafter? | They say every couple dreads that loss, tries to prepare responsibly, and can still expect a deep friendship to continue in the afterlife even though marriage as such does not. | He says married couples naturally fear a spouse's death and should plan responsibly so the survivor is cared for. In the hereafter, marriage as a sexual union ceases, but relationships continue; a faithful spouse may remain one of the closest friendships after one's relationship with Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=9349" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:49</a> | Thoughts on Micah 5:5-6. How does this relate to Jesus? | He treats the passage as part of the wider messianic prophecy in Micah 5, with the Messiah protecting and shepherding his people. | He reads Micah 5:5-6 in the context of the Bethlehem ruler prophecy beginning in verse 2. The Assyrian language, in his answer, depicts the Messiah as ruler, shepherd, protector, and warrior who defends his people if they are attacked. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=9622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:22</a> | How does Saul being king and Samuel being judge at the same time work? | He says Samuel still functioned as an arbiter of disputes while Saul handled kingship and warfare. | He says a judge was primarily an arbiter of justice and community disputes, not simply a king. Saul's kingship, especially his focus on Philistine wars, did not eliminate Samuel's role as a respected dispute-settler during the transition from judges to monarchy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=9770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:50</a> | If Saul was not a good king, why was he chosen? | He says Saul was exactly the kind of king Israel wanted, which helped show that what they wanted was not what they needed. | He says Israel wanted a king like the other nations, and Saul fit that desire: tall, confident, and impressive. God gave them the kind of king they asked for, thereby showing that what they wanted was not what they needed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=9912" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:12</a> | How do we know when the Bible is talking about Kish versus Kush? | He says the consonantal spelling overlaps, so context and geography have to determine which one is meant. | He says early biblical Hebrew lacked vowels, so Kish and Kush looked the same in consonantal spelling. The only way to distinguish them is context: African origin or geography points to Kush, while Mesopotamian or Syrian context points to Kish. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=10033" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:13</a> | What caused the large amounts of pain in Egypt? | He points to manual labor injuries, arthritis, dental disease, and other ordinary bodily suffering. | He lists manual labor, injury, arthritis, and dental pain as major causes of chronic suffering. He rejects the old "sand in bread" explanation for Egyptian dental disease, instead attributing many cavities to sticky, sugary dates beginning especially around the Amarna period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=10225" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:25</a> | Do any Egyptian mummies show evidence of cancer? | Yes. He says cancers do appear in mummies and are not especially rare. | He says Egyptian mummies do show evidence of several kinds of cancer, including bone pitting associated with advanced disease. He emphasizes that Egyptians suffered cancer like other people and that such evidence is not especially uncommon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=10271" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:11</a> | Who are the three kings you think might have reigned during the time of Joseph, and what do we know of them? | He names three likely 15th-dynasty candidates and says we know very little about them beyond sparse king lists and a few cartouches. | He names Benu, Apachnan, and Janassi Ad as likely 15th-dynasty candidates, with some neighboring possibilities before and after them. He says we know almost nothing beyond king-list notices and a few cartouches because the Second Intermediate Period preserves only a tiny amount of text, but the chariot evidence makes a 15th-dynasty setting more viable for him than Dynasty 14. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=10435" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:53:55</a> | What about Khayan and Joseph? | He says some overlap is possible, but that would push the chronology toward the outer edge of what he thinks is likely. | He says Khayan could possibly overlap Joseph, especially later in Joseph's tenure, but that scenario pushes the chronology toward the edge of what he finds likely. He treats it as possible rather than his preferred placement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=10482" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:42</a> | What was Rohl's Joseph statue supposed to be? | He says it is better understood as a 13th-dynasty noble or royal official, not as Joseph. | He says the statue Rohl identifies with Joseph dates firmly to Dynasty 13 and is probably a local Semitic prince, noble, or royal official. Because he places it in the late 19th or early 18th century BC, he says it predates the patriarchal timeframe too much to be Joseph, even if the multicolored tunic is suggestive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=10702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:22</a> | In Luke 15:1, what would denote someone as a sinner? | He says the label referred to people living in patterns of impurity or exclusion that marked them as pariahs from the assembly. | He says "sinners" in Luke 15:1 are not merely ordinary people who sin, but those whose life patterns excluded them from the assembly or temple precinct. He gives examples such as prostitutes, tax collectors, adulterers, and people violating purity or kosher expectations in ways that made them social and religious pariahs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=11078" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:38</a> | What is the deal with natal charts and zodiacs? Why do they seem so accurate? | He says they are vague enough for people to read themselves into them, rather than genuinely accurate. | He says zodiac and natal-chart claims seem accurate because people bring their own preconceptions and tolerate vague wording. He points to experiments where people identify with readings even when signs are swapped, concluding that the effect is ambiguity, not real predictive accuracy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=11395" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:55</a> | In Acts 2:1, were the believers with one accord and in one place, or just in one place? What does the Greek say? | He says this is a textual variant issue: some Byzantine witnesses include one accord, while more critical editions tend to preserve only one place. | He says Acts 2:1 involves a text-critical variant, not translators hiding the Greek. Byzantine or majority-text traditions include language about being of one accord and in one place, while modern critical editions tend to prefer the shorter "in one place" reading because of the manuscript evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=12053" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:53</a> | How should we interpret Ezekiel 1:4-28 in its description of angels? Is it literal? | He says it is not a straightforward literal description but an analogical human attempt to describe heavenly beings. | He says Ezekiel is describing heavenly spirit beings through analogical, culturally shaped human language. The prophet is not giving a simple literal anatomy of angels; he is trying to express a spiritual reality, and the human mind supplies familiar forms and images to make the vision speakable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=12589" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:49</a> | How many of your teachers say it came in a dream? | Very few. He treats dreams as an unreliable and potentially dangerous way to claim contact with spirits. | He says almost none of his teachers grounded claims that way. He warns that dreams are not a reliable means of contact with spirits because deceptive powers can imitate light, so he treats attempts to reach spiritual beings through dreams as spiritually dangerous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=12667" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:07</a> | Could you elaborate on how Amorites are important to the book of Joshua? I thought Amorites were used interchangeably with Canaanites. | He says they are distinct groups, with Amorites acting as major Bronze Age power brokers rather than simply another name for Canaanites. | He says Amorites and Canaanites are ethnically distinct, not interchangeable. Amorites migrated from northern Mesopotamia into the Levant, became major Late Bronze Age power brokers, conquered or dominated Canaanite city-states, and formed loose confederacies that matter for Joshua's setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=13117" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:38:37</a> | What is the psychology behind ABR accepting one Exodus date and then fudging data that does not fit? | He says many within that circle treat the issue as tied to salvation and therefore justify temporary distortion in service of what they think is a higher truth. | He says the psychology is an ends-justify-the-means mindset rooted in a very literal early-Exodus hermeneutic. In his account, some older ABR figures treated rejection of the early date as spiritually disastrous, so temporary data-fudging felt justified while they waited for future discoveries to vindicate them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=13354" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:42:34</a> | What is a gander, and why does it need sauce? | A gander is a male goose, and he says goose is usually basted and served with sauce because of how it cooks. | He says a gander is a male goose. Goose is fatty rather than dry, but it needs careful cooking so the fat drains and the skin crisps; the "sauce" is usually the basting fat or accompanying sauce used while preparing it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=13397" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:17</a> | Are you still considering joining their conference after people tried to recruit you? | He says he joined the NES and may give papers there, partly because he thinks the group needs dissenting voices. | He says he did join the NES after a friend convinced him there was no real harm. He sees the group as needing membership and dissenting academic voices, and he is willing to present papers there so they can critique his work and hear another side. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=13885" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:25</a> | Is there any research you are working on now that might become papers? | Yes. He says the Berlin pedestal is likely to become his next paper. | He says the Berlin pedestal is high on his priority list and will probably be his next paper, possibly his first paper for the NES. He wants to get that research finished and out. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14062" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:22</a> | Have you tried Peking duck? | Yes. He says he has tried it and likes both the dish and the fuller multi-course presentation. | He says he has tried Peking duck and finds it delicious. He distinguishes between ordering it as a central dish and the fuller multi-course presentation, where multiple dishes are made from different portions of the duck. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14114" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:14</a> | Where does goose fall on the poultry pricing spectrum? | He says it is pricier than common store birds like chicken or turkey, but still below very expensive game birds such as swan or pheasant. | He says goose is on the higher end of common domestic birds: more expensive than chicken, Cornish hen, or turkey, but cheaper than rarer birds such as pheasant, grouse, peacock, or swan. He estimates it is still a special but attainable purchase rather than the most expensive poultry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14313" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:58:33</a> | What is the ancient Near Eastern relationship between stars and angels? | He says ancient people saw the moving lights in the sky as alive and connected them analogically to heavenly powers. | He says "stars" in this context often means visible heavenly lights, especially planets. Since ancient observers saw them move, they treated them as living or as connected with spiritual powers, using analogical language that relates celestial lights to angels or heavenly beings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14367" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:27</a> | Have you tried alligator meat? | Yes. He says he likes it and describes it as having the texture of chicken breast with a fish-like taste. | He says he has tried alligator meat, liked it, and would eat it again. His description is that it has the texture of chicken breast but tastes like fish, and he recalls having it at the Gilroy Garlic Festival. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14436" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:00:36</a> | Is Satan a principality or a power? | He says Satan could fit the ruler category, but scripture identifies him more clearly as a throne cherub than by those later classificatory labels. | He says Satan might fit the "ruler" or archon category, but the biblical evidence is not precise enough to classify him neatly as a principality or power. What he thinks is clearer is that Ezekiel identifies Satan as a covering or throne cherub, and the later rank labels are less firmly defined. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14642" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:04:02</a> | Is Satan the prince of the power of the air in Ephesians 2:2? | Yes. He says that is one of Satan's titles. | He says yes, "prince of the power of the air" is a title for Satan, the serpent. He treats it as one of several descriptions rather than a complete taxonomy of Satan's rank. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14742" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:42</a> | What is the difference between a prince, principality, power, dominion, throne, and the host of heaven? | He sketches them as different ranks or functions of spiritual authority, with the host of heaven being the broad heavenly army under God. | He describes princes or principalities as rulers over domains, powers as beings with authority to act within spheres, dominions as areas of influence, and thrones as another ruler-like category. The host of heaven is broader: the heavenly army or forces under God's command. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=14964" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:24</a> | What are some underrated spirit critters? | He points especially to guardian spirits or guardian angels that do protective day-to-day work without the prominence of figures like Michael or Gabriel. | He points to guardian angels or guardian spirits as underrated because they do necessary, less glamorous work for people's benefit and salvation. He says scripture confirms such protectors exist, though it is unclear whether they are assigned to individuals or groups. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=15192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:13:12</a> | Why did Israel repeatedly worship other gods when they had direct encounters with God, miracles, and theophanies? | He says Israel still acted like sinners and treated other gods as an insurance policy when they failed to trust God fully. | He says Israel's recurring idolatry came from sin, lack of trust, and a desire for fallback protection. Even with more divine encounters than other nations, they did not experience God every moment, so they chased other gods as spiritual insurance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=15321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:15:21</a> | Why did David marry so many women? Was it out of spite over Saul's daughter? | He says the marriages were mainly political treaty marriages, not chiefly about spite or libido. | He says David's many marriages were mostly political, reflecting how kings used wives to make treaties and build alliances. He adds that such royal households were expensive and that God did not intend Israel's kings to multiply wives or treaties in that way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/JCX5-10cRnY?t=15654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:20:54</a> | In Isaiah 60, will rulers and kings exist in the new earth? | Yes. He says the new earth still includes nations and authority structures, though exercised for right order rather than oppression. | He says the new earth will include nations, kings, rulers, and judges. Their authority will not be oppressive; it will order good-faith disputes, guide people rightly, and participate under Christ's rule, with the rod of iron understood as a scepter of authority rather than a tool of abuse. |
